critique.summary: The article discusses the Baochip-1x, a new RISC-V microcontroller featuring a Memory Management Unit (MMU), which is uncommon in its class. The author explains the significance of the MMU for running high-assurance software and the evolution of microcontroller design over the years.
mainPoints: The Baochip-1x includes a Memory Management Unit (MMU), a feature not commonly found in similar microcontrollers.
The MMU allows for secure application management and is essential for running complex operating systems like Linux.
critique.truthfulness: The claims made in the article regarding the uniqueness of the MMU in the Baochip-1x compared to other microcontrollers in its class appear to be accurate. The historical context provided about the evolution of microcontrollers and the limitations of earlier designs is also well-founded. However, the assertion that no other microcontroller in this performance/integration class has an MMU should be verified against the latest market offerings.
considerations: The article does not mention any competing microcontrollers that may also feature an MMU.
Potential market reception and practical applications of the Baochip-1x are not discussed.

comments_critique.summary: The discussion revolves around the Baochip-1x project, with users expressing excitement, asking questions, and sharing insights about the technology and its implications. Several comments highlight the contributions of the author, Bunnie, to the hardware hacking community.

comments.author: bunnie
comments.text: Hello wonderful people! I'm bunnie - just noticed this is on HN. Unfortunately due to timezones I'm about to afk for a bit.
comments.analysis: Bunnie's comment establishes a welcoming tone and indicates a willingness to engage with the community, which is constructive.

comments.author: luma
comments.text: bunnie your book "Hacking the XBox" taught me how to get started on reversing electronics, took the fear out of the process...
comments.analysis: This comment expresses gratitude and highlights the impact of Bunnie's work, adding a personal touch to the discussion.

comments.author: stavros
comments.text:
Wait a minute, why can't I reply to bunnie's top-level comment? Anyway, here's what I wanted to say:

Adding your CPU to another company's silicon is a genius move...
comments.analysis: The comment provides a thoughtful observation about the project, although the initial complaint about replying detracts slightly from its focus.

comments.author: genxy
comments.text: To anyone from crowdsupply listening, please turn down your VPN check. I am not stripping my privacy protection to use your site.
comments.analysis: This comment raises a valid concern about user privacy, although it is somewhat off-topic from the main discussion about the project.

comments.author: vintagedave
comments.text:
This is wonderful! Also what a fantastic partnership that allowed adding a new CPU to that die. Kudos to them.

I had a lot of trouble finding out which open source license applies...
comments.analysis: While the comment is positive and acknowledges the project's success, it shifts focus to a licensing question that may not be central to the discussion.

comments.author: bArray
comments.text: > Those with a bit of silicon savvy would note that it’s not cheap to produce such a chip, yet, I have not raised a dollar of venture capital...
comments.analysis: This comment asks relevant questions about the project's financial aspects, but lacks a deeper engagement with the topic at hand.

comments.author: hedgehog
comments.text:
Bunnie did a really good talk a couple months ago that has more of the background beyond what's in the blog post:

...
comments.analysis: The comment points to additional resources, which is helpful, but it doesn't contribute new insights about the Baochip-1x itself.

comments.author: alexisread
comments.text:
Great work on the chip, I’m really onboard with the trusted computing aim!

Is there a way to bootstrap binary code into the reram?
comments.analysis: This comment shows support for the project but poses a technical question that may not be directly addressed in the original post.

comments.author: mijoharas
comments.text:
Cool project. Why is it called the Baochip/Dabao?

Is it big Bao? Or take-away (just learnt the second meaning), or something else?
comments.analysis: The comment raises an interesting question about the project's name, though it doesn't engage deeply with the technical aspects.

comments.author: awesomeusername
comments.text:
I run a hardware company now (thankfully in the age of AI), as a direct consequence of reading Bunnies book 'hardware hacker'

Thank you Bunnie.
comments.analysis: While this comment expresses appreciation, it is more about personal experience than the Baochip-1x project itself.

comments.author: chuckadams
comments.text:
> What’s a banker going to do with the source code of a chip, anyway?

Hand it to someone who does know what to do with it...
comments.analysis: This comment attempts to address a question but does so in a somewhat dismissive manner, which may not foster constructive dialogue.

comments.author: the_biot
comments.text: Why the few closed-source components on the system? You mention the bus, USB PHY etc -- are those things harder to design than the CPU core?
comments.analysis: This comment raises a relevant question but lacks context about the overall project, making it less impactful.

comments.author: gzread
comments.text: This is about transparency just like the Precursor, right? How can I know that my Baochip-1x is really what it says it is?
comments.analysis: While the comment addresses transparency, it may come off as skeptical without contributing positively to the discussion.

comments.author: K0balt
comments.text: Very cool! So there’s 5x riscV cores available?
comments.analysis: This comment expresses enthusiasm but lacks depth and clarity regarding the specifics of the project.

comments.author: intrasight
comments.text: I didn't know there were partially open source RISC-V. I might have missed it in the article, but what was the reason for having some parts closed source?
comments.analysis: This comment raises a valid point but does so in a way that suggests a lack of understanding of the project's details.

comments.author: arj
comments.text: It seems it had hardware support for secure mesh. Anyone know what that is?
comments.analysis: The comment asks a question but does not engage with the main topic, making it less relevant.

comments.author: Dani99
comments.analysis: This comment appears to be off-topic and does not contribute to the discussion about the Baochip-1x project.
